schools.summary: The shipwrecked company, having escaped the sea, came ashore and learned the island was Melita (Malta), learning its name only after landing — the fulfillment of God's promise (27:24) that all 276 would be saved, not one lost.
Henry: the providence — the company cast upon Malta, where they would be kindly received and where Paul's ministry would be displayed.

themes: when they were escaped
the island was called Melita
the safe landing on Malta
the fulfillment of God's promise of preservation

schools.summary: The 'barbarous people' (simply non-Greek-speaking foreigners, not savages, Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson; speaking a Punic dialect) showed no little kindness — kindling a fire and receiving the whole drenched, cold company with remarkable hospitality, all 276 welcomed.

themes: the barbarous people shewed us no little kindness
they kindled a fire, and received us every one
the kindness of the Maltese
humanity among the heathen

schools.summary: Paul, gathering a bundle of sticks (his humility and diligence, sharing the common labor), laid them on the fire, and a viper, roused by the heat, came out and fastened on his hand (Robertson) — the venomous serpent's deadly bite, the occasion for a display of God's power.

themes: Paul had gathered a bundle of sticks
there came a viper out of the heat, and fastened on his hand
Paul's humility
the sudden peril of the viper

schools.summary: The natives, seeing the viper hang on Paul (a prisoner), judged him a murderer pursued by divine Vengeance (Dike, the pagan goddess of Justice, Barnes, Clarke, Robertson) — punished though he escaped the sea, struck in the hand, the instrument of murder; reasoning from a true principle (God punishes sin) but applying it falsely.

Calvin: the conviction that calamity signals God's wrath is implanted in all (even barbarians) and not wholly false; but the error is to judge every sufferer guilty (as Job's friends did), forgetting God afflicts the godly too — we must suspend judgment and wait for the end.

debates.question: What does the natives' inference reveal — and where does it err?

sides.position: A true, implanted sense that God punishes sin, but corrupted by judging every sufferer guilty from outward calamity (the error of Job's friends)

themes: no doubt this man is a murderer
vengeance suffereth not to live
the natives' superstition
the implanted sense of divine justice, falsely applied

schools.summary: Paul calmly shook the viper into the fire (apotinasso, Robertson) and felt no harm — the remarkable fulfillment of Christ's promise (Mark 16:18; Luke 10:19), the venom powerless over him (Barnes, Lightfoot noting it among the signs).
Calvin: the token of a quiet mind — faith does not remove the feeling of danger but tempers it, so Paul, trusting God's promise, was untroubled, ready to die if need be.

themes: he shook off the beast into the fire
felt no harm
the fulfillment of Mark 16:18
Paul's calm faith and preservation

schools.summary: The natives, expecting Paul to swell up or drop dead from the venom (Barnes, Clarke, Robertson), saw no harm and swung to the opposite extreme — from murderer to god; the parallel with Lystra (14:11), 'so fickle is popular favour' (Robertson).
Calvin: the human bent to superstition — robbing God of his glory by deifying creatures; Wesley: the medium they missed, neither murderer nor god, but a man of God.

themes: they looked when he should have swollen
changed their minds, and said that he was a god
the fickle reversal from murderer to god
the parallel with Lystra

schools.summary: Publius, 'the chief man of the island' (protos, an actual title of the Maltese governor attested by inscriptions, a mark of Luke's accuracy, Clarke, Ellicott, Robertson), received and lodged the company courteously for three days.

Calvin: the providence and reward — Publius, perhaps moved by the miracle, honored Paul, and his hospitality would be richly repaid by the healing of his father (v.8); God rewarding kindness to strangers.

themes: the chief man of the island, whose name was Publius
lodged us three days courteously
the hospitality of Publius
Luke's accurate official title

schools.summary: Publius's father, sick of fevers and dysentery (Luke's medical precision, the plural indicating recurrent attacks, a critical case, Robertson, Ellicott, Clarke), was healed by Paul, who prayed and laid hands on him — the union reminding of James 5:14-15 and the juxtaposition with Mark 16:18.
Calvin: Paul, by prayer, gave God the glory — humbly craving the man's life of God, professing himself only the minister, not the author of the miracle.

themes: the father of Publius lay sick of a fever and of a bloody flux
prayed, and laid his hands on him, and healed him
the gift of healing through prayer
Paul the minister, not the author

schools.summary: After the healing of Publius's father, a continuing stream of the island's sick came and were healed (the imperfect tense, the article, Robertson, Ellicott) — a wider ministry of healing; the possible distinction between iaomai (Paul's miracles) and therapeuo (the cures).
Clarke: Luke the physician was not even consulted — Paul entered, prayed, laid on hands, and healed; miraculous power, not medical art.

themes: others also, which had diseases, came, and were healed
the wider ministry of healing on Malta
the power of God confirming the gospel
Luke the physician not consulted

schools.summary: The grateful Maltese honored the company with many honors (timais, often used for an honorarium, Robertson) and, at their departure, supplied them with all needful things for the voyage — gratitude and provision repaying the healings, the company furnished for the rest of the journey.

themes: honoured us with many honours
laded us with such things as were necessary
the gratitude of the Maltese
the providence furnishing the company

schools.summary: After three months wintering on Malta (navigation suspended in the stormy season), the company departed in an Alexandrian grain ship that had wintered there, bearing the figurehead of Castor and Pollux (the Twin Brothers, the Dioscuri, sons of Zeus, patrons of sailors, Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson).
Henry: the irony — the ship bore the sign of the pagan twin gods, yet it was the true God who protected Paul.

themes: after three months we departed in a ship of Alexandria
whose sign was Castor and Pollux
the resumption of the voyage
the pagan figurehead, the true God protecting

schools.summary: The company landed at Syracuse (the chief city of Sicily, a notable seaport about 80 miles from Malta) and tarried three days — perhaps for trade or favorable winds, the natural route of the Alexandrian ship toward Italy; the steady, providential progress toward Rome.

themes: landing at Syracuse, we tarried three days
the first leg of the resumed voyage
the chief city of Sicily
the steady advance toward Rome

schools.summary: The ship made a circuit (tacked, Robertson) to Rhegium (on the toe of Italy, opposite Sicily), then, with a favorable south wind, reached Puteoli (Pozzuoli, near Naples, the chief port of southern Italy where the Alexandrian grain ships landed) the next day — the arrival in Italy, the effective end of the sea voyage.

themes: we fetched a compass, and came to Rhegium
the south wind blew, and we came to Puteoli
the arrival in Italy
the favorable wind speeding the company

schools.summary: At Puteoli the company found brethren (an established church, the gospel already spread to this Italian port) who invited them to stay seven days — the centurion Julius (favorably disposed) allowing the week's stay (Robertson), giving Paul fellowship before the final overland stage to Rome.

themes: where we found brethren
desired to tarry with them seven days
the fellowship at Puteoli
the approach to Rome

schools.summary: The Roman Christians, hearing of Paul's arrival, came out on the Appian Way to meet him — some to Appii Forum (40 miles from Rome), some to the Three Taverns (30 miles, Barnes, Clarke, Robertson); and Paul, long desiring to see the Roman church (Romans 1:9-11), thanked God and took fresh courage (cf. 23:11) at their love.
Calvin: the value of fellowship — God comforted Paul through the brethren, that he might more joyfully defend the gospel; the godly are strengthened by means of one another.

themes: the brethren came to meet us as far as Appii forum, and the three taverns
he thanked God, and took courage
the Roman brethren's love
the comfort of fellowship

schools.summary: At Rome the prisoners were delivered to the captain of the Praetorian Guard (perhaps Burrus, a note of time, Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott), but Paul was allowed to dwell by himself (a custodia libera, in his own lodging, chained to a single soldier, Robertson, Ellicott) — the favor due to Julius's good report and Festus's testimony of his innocence.

Calvin: the providence — God governed Paul's bonds, granting him liberty not merely to ease his trouble but that the faithful might have free access and the treasure of the gospel not be shut up in prison.

themes: when we came to Rome
Paul was suffered to dwell by himself with a soldier
the goal reached, Christ's promise kept (23:11)
the providential liberty for the gospel

schools.summary: Paul, within three days of arriving at Rome, called the chief Jews together (his unwearied love for Israel, ever beginning with his own nation) and declared his innocence — nothing committed against the Jewish people or the customs of the fathers, yet delivered a prisoner to the Romans, stating his case without bitterness.
Chrysostom (with Henry): Paul's meekness and wisdom — conciliating the Jews, addressing them as brethren, stating his case mildly to gain a hearing for the gospel.

themes: Paul called the chief of the Jews together
I have committed nothing against the people, or customs
Paul's prompt outreach to the Jews
the temperate defense

schools.summary: Paul appealed to the Roman examination — the authorities (Felix, Festus, and the hearing before Agrippa, 26:31-32) had found no capital charge against him and would have released him, there being no cause of death (Barnes, Robertson); to show the Jews his bonds were not for any crime.

themes: when they had examined me, would have let me go
no cause of death in me
the Roman verdict of innocence
Paul's vindication

schools.summary: When the Jews opposed his release (seeking his death), Paul was constrained to appeal to Caesar to save his life (25:11, Barnes, Robertson) — yet not to accuse his nation, his love for Israel undimmed, no charge brought against them.
Wesley (with Calvin): Paul's meekness — even in appealing to Caesar, he bore no grudge against his people, seeking not their harm but his own safety.

themes: the Jews spake against it, I was constrained to appeal unto Caesar
not that I had ought to accuse my nation
the appeal forced by the Jews
Paul's tender care for Israel

schools.summary: Paul declared the true ground of his bonds — 'the hope of Israel,' the two-fold hope of the Messiah and of the resurrection of the dead (both grounded in Christ, the resurrection of Jesus proving him the Messiah, Romans 1:3-4, Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott, Robertson); bound with a single chain (the custodia libera), a touch of pathos.
Calvin: Paul is bound for the hope of Israel because the whole hope of the godly rests in Christ — no man hopes aright but he who looks to Christ and his spiritual kingdom.

themes: for the hope of Israel I am bound with this chain
the Messiah and the resurrection
the true ground of Paul's bonds
Christ the hope of Israel

schools.summary: The Roman Jews replied that no letters from Judaea and no traveler had brought any charge against Paul — perhaps because the Jerusalem Jews saw their cause as hopeless (the winter suspending travel, or their abandoning the prosecution, Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson); Wesley notes the peculiar providence, the bridle in the enemies' mouths.
Calvin: their contempt or despair — not gentleness toward Paul, but because they could not oppress him so far off, his removal to Rome seeming to them as good as a grave.

themes: we neither received letters out of Judaea concerning thee
neither spake any harm of thee
the absence of accusation
the providence restraining Paul's enemies

schools.summary: The Roman Jews requested to hear Paul's views, thinking it fair to hear his side (Robertson), identifying him with 'this sect' — Christianity, everywhere spoken against (maligned and slandered, as Tacitus and Suetonius attest, Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott).
Wesley: the irony — 'this is no proof at all of a bad cause, but a very probable mark of a good one'; the world's hostility no condemnation of the truth.

themes: we desire to hear of thee what thou thinkest
this sect every where spoken against
the maligned reputation of the gospel
opposition no mark against the truth

schools.summary: On the appointed day, many came to Paul's lodging, and he expounded and testified the kingdom of God and persuaded concerning Jesus — from the Law and the Prophets, the Scriptures the Jews accepted, from morning till evening (Robertson), a full day's labor; Henry notes the Jews' willingness, more than at Jerusalem.

Calvin: Paul defined the kingdom rightly against carnal Jewish notions — spiritual, beginning in newness of life, ending in heavenly glory; and persuaded them to receive Christ, the suffering Redeemer foretold by the prophets, all from the pure fountain of the word.

themes: he expounded and testified the kingdom of God
persuading them concerning Jesus, out of the law and the prophets
the full day's exposition
the spiritual kingdom and the Christ

schools.summary: Some believed, some believed not — 'some began to be persuaded, some continued to disbelieve' (the imperfect tense, Robertson), the usual divided result of gospel preaching; the cross and the spiritual kingdom the stumbling-block (Clarke).
Calvin: even Paul's grace-filled preaching could not win all — the unbelief of many resists the plainest doctrine, for faith is not common to all; those return blind who came willingly.

themes: some believed, and some believed not
the divided response to the gospel
belief and unbelief
the sovereignty of grace

schools.summary: The Jews, divided and discordant, departed; and Paul spoke one last solemn word — 'Well spake the Holy Ghost by Esaias' — ascribing the prophecy to the Holy Ghost speaking through Isaiah (a strong testimony to inspiration, Barnes, Robertson), showing the Jews they resist God himself (cf. 7:51).

Calvin: Paul, seeing their obstinacy, inveighs sharply, citing the Holy Ghost to give the oracle authority — the stubborn must be cited to God's judgment-seat; by naming the Spirit, Paul shows the word proceeds from God, not man's brain.

themes: well spake the Holy Ghost by Esaias the prophet
the Jews agreed not, and departed
the inspiration of Scripture
resisting the Spirit of God

schools.summary: Paul quotes Isaiah 6:9 — 'hearing ye shall hear, and shall not understand' — the prophecy of judicial blindness, cited six times in the New Testament (Robertson, Calvin), applied by Christ to his parables and by John and Paul to the Jews' rejection of the Messiah; a wilful blindness consequent on resisting the truth (Wesley).

Calvin: the prophet attributes the blindness to God's secret judgment, yet the people shut their own eyes — both are true; God blinds the reprobate, yet they become blind of their own accord, the word's clarity leaving them inexcusable.

debates.question: Is the blindness God's judgment or the people's own self-hardening?

sides.position: Both — God's secret judgment and the people's wilful shutting of their own eyes, leaving them inexcusable

themes: hearing ye shall hear, and shall not understand
seeing ye shall see, and not perceive
the prophecy of judicial blindness
God's judgment and man's self-hardening

schools.summary: The heart of the people waxed gross (stupefied, hardened against spiritual truth, Gill), their ears dull, their eyes closed — a wilful refusal, lest they should see, hear, understand, be converted, and healed; the people, by their own hardening, cut off from the conversion and healing God would give.

themes: the heart of this people is waxed gross
their eyes have they closed, lest they should be converted
the hardened heart and its tragic consequence
mercy forfeited by self-hardening

schools.summary: Paul, the Jews having rejected the gospel, solemnly declares (as at Antioch, 13:46, and Corinth, 18:6) that the salvation of God is sent to the Gentiles, and they will hear it — the emphatic 'they' contrasting the receptive Gentiles with the rejecting Jews (Robertson); Paul's unwavering faith offering the gospel where it would be received (Barnes); the climax of Acts.

Calvin: salvation sent to the Gentiles in the second place, after the Jews rejected it, not indiscriminately but to provoke Israel to jealousy (Romans 11:11; Deuteronomy 32:21); the doctrine others refuse profiting the nations.

themes: the salvation of God is sent unto the Gentiles
they will hear it
the climactic turning to the Gentiles
the great theme of Acts consummated

schools.summary: The Jews departed and reasoned warmly among themselves — the believing party contending with the unbelieving (Barnes, Clarke) — though the whole verse is textually doubtful, wanting in many early manuscripts and likely a later insertion to ease the transition from v.28 to v.30 (Barnes, Ellicott).
Calvin: the wicked, nettled by the prophecy, disputed; yet some embraced Paul's words and defended them stoutly — for if we will have peace with God, we must war with those who contemn him.

debates.question: Is verse 29 part of the original text?

sides.position: Textually doubtful — wanting in many early manuscripts and versions, likely a later insertion or revised reading

themes: the Jews departed, and had great reasoning among themselves
the divided dispute
the textual question
contention advancing the truth

schools.summary: Paul dwelt two whole years in his own hired lodging (with companions, guarded by a soldier, Barnes) and received all who came — Jews and Gentiles alike — a settled, open, fruitful ministry; during which he wrote several epistles (Ephesians, Philippians, Colossians, Philemon) and converted many, even in Caesar's household (Philippians 1).
Henry: the providence — even a prisoner, Paul had a wide and open door, receiving all who came.

themes: Paul dwelt two whole years in his own hired house
received all that came in unto him
the settled, fruitful ministry at Rome
the open door of the prisoner-apostle

schools.summary: Paul preached the kingdom of God and taught the things concerning the Lord Jesus Christ (the sum of the gospel, Clarke, Barnes), with all confidence (parresia, full boldness and liberty of speech) and no man forbidding him — the Roman empire as yet tolerant (Ellicott), the gospel proclaimed unhindered at the capital of the world; the abrupt close perhaps because Luke wrote at the end of the two years or Theophilus knew the sequel (Ellicott).

themes: preaching the kingdom of God, teaching the things concerning the Lord Jesus Christ
with all confidence, no man forbidding him
the triumphant, unhindered close of Acts
the gospel proclaimed at the capital of the world
